# Vertical Integration Strategies of the Top Three Global Cocoa Processors (2018–2024)
## Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam/ofi: Upstream Investment, EUDR Compliance, and Premium-Chocolate Competitive Positioning

---

## Executive Summary

The three largest industrial cocoa processors—Barry Callebaut (BC), Cargill, and Olam Food Ingredients (ofi, formerly Olam Cocoa)—together process approximately half of the world's cocoa beans, with the top five processors (adding Fuji Oil/Blommer and Guan Chong) accounting for roughly 55–60% of global grindings capacity (https://www.dol.gov/sites/dolgov/files/ILAB/Cocoa%20SC%20Report_508%20compliant.pdf). Between 2018 and 2024, all three pursued a convergent vertical-integration strategy combining direct cooperative sourcing, expanded in-country processing capacity, and aggressive farm-level polygon mapping in anticipation of the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR, Regulation 2023/1115). The practical effect has been a shift from a certification-led sustainability posture (Rainforest Alliance/UTZ, Cocoa Promise, Cocoa Compass) to a plot-level digital-traceability posture that is now a prerequisite for EU-bound premium chocolate contracts with Lindt, Ferrero, and Mondelēz. The EU Commission in late 2024 proposed a 12-month postponement of the original 30 December 2024 application date, with first obligations for large operators and traders now expected from 30 December 2025 (https://publications.europa.eu/resource/cellar/693156f0-5d3b-11f0-a9d0-01aa75ed71a1.0001.01/DOC_1).

---

## 1. Market Structure and Concentration

The six largest branded chocolate manufacturers—Nestlé, Ferrero, Mars, Mondelēz, Hershey, and Lindt & Sprüngli—together transform roughly 40% of the world's chocolate, providing a concentrated downstream pull that has shaped processor investment behaviour. Cocoa-bean price inflation through 2023–2024 further rewarded origin-country processing: BC's FY 2023/24 sales rose 28.1% in local currencies to CHF 10.4 billion, though free cash flow turned sharply negative at CHF −2,330.7 million (https://au.marketscreener.com/quote/stock/BARRY-CALLEBAUT-AG-408710/news/Barry-Callebaut-Annual-Report-2023-24-Barry-Callebaut-Annual-Report-2023-24-48267986/).

---

## 2. Barry Callebaut

### 2.1 Direct vs. Broker Sourcing

Independent supply-chain mapping by Trase (2024) estimates that BC sources approximately 86% of its cocoa directly from cooperatives in Côte d'Ivoire, with the remainder acquired through indirect/intermediary channels (https://trase.earth/insights/cote-d-ivoire-cocoa-exports-and-deforestation-2026). BC's own public traceability map confirms 100% direct-chain traceability to the cooperative/warehouse level, and approximately 70,000 additional farmers in its indirect supply are mapped (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/sustainability/our-sustainable-raw-materials/transparency-and-traceability-our-cocoa-supply-chain). In Côte d'Ivoire, the company has signed three-year contracts with 90% of the cooperatives from which it directly sources (https://esgdistrict.lecho.be/media/Barry-Callebaut-2023-ENG-1.pdf).

### 2.2 Origin-Country Capital Expenditures

- Côte d'Ivoire (March 2019): Inauguration of the new SACO (Société Africaine de Cacao) Zone 4C grinding unit in Abidjan, part of a CHF 55 million (~USD 56 million) five-year investment to lift SACO capacity by +40% by 2022, creating 45 direct and 120 indirect jobs. Key Ivorian grinding plants are at Abidjan and San Pedro (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/about-us/media/news-stories/barry-callebaut-expands-its-cocoa-processing-capacities-cote-divoire).
- Ghana (July 2022): Partnership with Empower New Energy to expand solar PV at the Tema factory to 0.64 MWp (phase 2), on top of an earlier 0.5 MW solar farm. The Tema Free Zone Enclave plant, originally opened in 2001 and doubled to 60,000 t/year in 2006, remains operational (https://thecocoapost.com/barry-callebaut-ghana-signs-deal-to-increase-solar-power-for-cocoa-processing/).

### 2.3 Sustainability Programs and Farmer Income

The Forever Chocolate strategy (launched 2016) and the Cocoa Horizons Foundation (established 2015) are the umbrella vehicles, with activity spanning Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Cameroon, Indonesia, Nigeria, Ecuador, and Brazil. Disclosed farmer-income data include:

- 557,739 farmers in BC's supply chain are now above the World Bank extreme poverty line (2024/25 update aligned to USD 3.00/person/day) (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/sustainability/reporting/forever-chocolate-progress-report-202425).
- The independent Agri-Logic study (2023) found that 2021/22 was the highest farm-income year since 2018/19 at ~XOF 1.35 million (~USD 2,200) per farm, with cocoa representing 83% of income; <10% of farmers produced yields high enough to reach a living income, and ~60% produced <600 kg/ha (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/system/files/2023-05/Barry%20Callebaut%20Agrilogic%20White%20Paper%202023_1.pdf).
- Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana raised the official farm-gate price to XOF 2,200/kg and GHS 33.12/kg respectively in 2024/25 in response to the supply shock (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/sustainability/strategy/Prospering-Farmers).
- Cocoa Horizons reported CHF 19.4 million in premium income for FY 2023/24, with total program income of CHF 45.2 million (https://www.cocoahorizons.org/sites/default/files/2026-03/The%20Cocoa%20Snapshot%20Full%20Year%2023-24_1.pdf).

### 2.4 EUDR Compliance and Traceability

As of 2024/25, BC has mapped over 1.5 million cocoa farms, claims full farm-level traceability for cocoa sourced directly for the European market, and reports 100% deforestation-free sourcing for volumes supplied to its Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana facilities within EUDR scope (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/system/files/2025-11/Annual%20Social%20and%20Environmental%20Impact%20Report%202024_25.pdf). The mapping rate inside the direct supply chain rose from 66.0% (FY 2019/20) to 85.6% (2024/25), while deforestation-free verified volumes rose from 24.5% to 81.8% (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/system/files/2025-11/ESG%20Datasheet%202024_25_0.pdf). BC has been the most vocal EUDR advocate among the three, publicly opposing the 12-month delay in November 2024 and July 2025 (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/about-us/media/news-stories/statement-eudr). The Callebaut Finest Belgian line has been 100% cocoa-bean traceable via QR code since 2019 (https://www.callebaut.com/en/callebaut-and-eudr).

---

## 3. Cargill

### 3.1 Direct vs. Broker Sourcing

Cargill's 2024 Impact Report states the company "directly sources cocoa from six producing countries: Brazil, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, Ecuador, Ghana, and Indonesia" and "indirectly" from 19 additional origins, claiming to be the "second-largest producer of cocoa and chocolate globally" (https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271059750/2024-impact-report-cocoa.pdf). In Ghana, direct sourcing is conducted through Cargill's own licensed buying company Cargill Kokoo Sourcing Limited (CKSL), established 2016, with e-money payments via MTN Mobile Money (https://graphic.com.gh/news/general-news/cargill-delivers-more-cocoa-sustainability-benefits-pays-gh-6-million-to-cocoa-farmers.html). The dedicated "Promise Cocoa" sub-supply chain represents "about one third of the cocoa we source" (KPMG-audited) (https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271059750/2024-impact-report-cocoa.pdf).

### 3.2 Origin-Country Capital Expenditures

- Côte d'Ivoire (December 2019): USD 100 million Yopougon plant expansion, +50% capacity (now the single largest cocoa-grinding plant in Africa), creating 85 direct and hundreds of indirect jobs (https://www.cargill.com/2019/cargill-invests-$113-million-in-ivory-coast-and-ghana).
- Ghana (same December 2019 announcement): USD 13 million for a +20% expansion of the Tema plant, commissioned November 2021, raising Tema's annual capacity to 90,000 tonnes and adding ~130 permanent/contracted staff to a workforce that grew from 400 to 530 (https://www.cargill.com/2021/cargill-commissions-us$13-million-cocoa-processing-plant).
- Additional investments: USD 12.3 million sustainability/traceability co-investment (USD 7.7 million CIV, USD 4.6 million Ghana) and a follow-on USD 13 million for Ivorian farmer programming, bringing total disclosed capex for 2019–2021 to approximately USD 226 million (https://www.cargill.com/2021/cargill-completes-us$100m-cocoa-processing-expansion).

### 3.3 Sustainability Programs and Farmer Income

The Cargill Cocoa Promise, launched 2012 in West Africa, covers Brazil, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, Ecuador, Ghana, and Indonesia, reaching 222,000 farmers and 660 farmer organizations by the 10-year mark (October 2023) (https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/cocoa/the-cargill-cocoa-promise-10-year-anniversary). Disclosed farmer-income data include:

- 2017/18 season: USD 870,000 in premiums in Ghana, distributed to 13,000 farmers (https://www.prnewswire.co.uk/news-releases/cargill-doubles-sustainable-sourced-cocoa-in-ghana-benefitting-13000-farmers-692408351.html).
- 2018/19 and 2019/20 seasons: GH¢6 million each year, distributed to 19,000+ farmers in Ghana; 30,000 farmers in 10 districts covered (https://www.myjoyonline.com/cargill-cocoa-promise-delivers-%c2%a26m-cocoa-sustainability-payments-to-ghanaian-cocoa-farmers/).
- Academic study (Frontiers in Sustainable Food Systems, 2021): Cargill Cocoa Promise cooperative members receiving a 35 CFA/kg cash premium and individual coaching had the highest mean and median cocoa income (per person per day) of all programs studied (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/sustainable-food-systems/articles/10.3389/fsufs.2021.732831/full).
- Ghana PROCOCO project: cocoa farmer income rose from USD 72 to USD 239 (a 3.3× increase) and access to financial services rose from 14% to 79% (https://www.cargill.com/doc/1432227748042/a-decade-of-impact-in-cocoa-communities.pdf).
- 2025 target: enable 25,000 Ivorian farming households to close or reduce their living-income gap by >50%.

### 3.4 EUDR Compliance and Traceability

The 2024 Impact Report shows polygon-mapping rates within the Promise supply chain:

- Côte d'Ivoire: 143,554 farms (99.9% mapped); 130,983 fully mapped (99.9%).
- Ghana: 81,406 farms (96.0% mapped); 30,302 fully mapped (93.1%).
- Cameroon: 32,097 farms (65.1% mapped); 20,944 fully mapped (62.6%).
- Total: 257,856 farms (92.2% mapped); 183,027 fully mapped (91.9%) (https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271058843/2024-impact-report-sustainable-supply-chain.pdf).

Cargill states it has "activated additional measures in Ghana and CIV to exclude, as much as possible, farmers not having all their cocoa plots polygon mapped or showing signs of deforestation since December 2014" from its sourcing—a concrete non-compliant-volume exclusion mechanism (https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1451373894196/2025-cargill-impact-report-cocoa.pdf). Technology partners include Farmforce/Koltiva (polygon mapping), Satelligence (satellite monitoring), WRI Global Forest Watch, and Meridia (on-the-ground verification). For EUDR, Cargill began a two-phase project in early 2023 (gap analysis → management system) and uses CocoaWise™ for customer reporting (https://www.cargill.com/food-beverage/emea/news/almost-ready-for-eudr-inside-our-compliance-preparations).

---

## 4. ofi (Olam Food Ingredients / Olam Cocoa)

### 4.1 Direct vs. Broker Sourcing

In 2020, ofi achieved 100% traceability in its direct global supply chain (≈569,104 t in 2020/21—approximately two-thirds of its total cocoa supply), enabling the company to trace roughly 12% of the world's cocoa beans to a farm, cooperative, or community (https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/olam-cocoa-hits-100-percent-traceability-target-across-its-direct-global-supply-chain.html). The remaining ~40% is sourced indirectly from other producers. In Ghana, ofi operates as a Licensed Buying Company sourcing 25% of all cocoa beans traded in the country, from approximately 120,000 farmers across Ashanti, Western North, Western South, Brong Ahafo, Central, and Eastern regions (https://www.fairlabor.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Baseline-Assessment_Assessing-working-conditions-in-cocoa-supply-chain-in-Ghana_Final.pdf).

### 4.2 Origin-Country Capital Expenditures

- Côte d'Ivoire (March 2015): San Pedro Olam Cocoa Processing (OCP) plant inaugurated by President Ouattara. USD 75 million investment, 75,000 t/year grind capacity, 450 local employees. Steam generated from cocoa-shell biomass plus 72 solar panels. Financing included a EUR 33.6 million FMO/Proparco/DEG facility inside a EUR 50 million total project (https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/ouattara-olam-cocoa-processing-facility-cote-divoire.html; https://www.fmo.nl/project-detail/32467).
- Côte d'Ivoire (2024): Olam Agri announced a USD 200 million investment to expand cocoa grinding capacity to support cocoa liquor, butter, and powder production for export to Europe, Asia, and North America (https://www.billionaireroom.com/471/).
- Ghana: Specific Ghana capex 2018–2024 was not publicly itemized in available disclosures; however, ofi's Cocoa Compass report confirms continuous investment in farm-level digital systems, and the 2025 CFI report notes the rollout of the ofi Direct (ofi D) digital first-mile traceability platform in Ghana (https://assets.ctfassets.net/yzlb24ni1fxz/1pulqVam4Shl15FJUUKqQc/1acb2330ecfd9eac25274938c0920335/ofi_2025_CFI_Report.pdf).

### 4.3 Sustainability Programs and Farmer Income

The Cocoa Compass framework (launched 2019) sets 2030 targets: 150,000 farmers at a living income, 100% child-labour monitoring, and a net-positive tree-carbon stock. The Côte d'Ivoire extreme-poverty line is set at USD 2,300/year and the living-income line at USD 7,300/year (Anker methodology) (https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/cocoa-compass-olam-cocoa-commits-to-living-incomes-for-farmers.html). Disclosed metrics include:

- 30,168 farmers in ofi's cocoa supply chain earning a living income as of 2024 (on the way to a 150,000 target by 2030) (https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/ofi-cocoa-shares-how-transparency-and-traceability-are-driving-sustainability-impact-for-food-and-beverage-sector.html).
- 9.8 million trees distributed for agroforestry from 2018 to 2024.
- 100% deforestation monitoring on directly sourced farms since 2021.
- Child Labour Monitoring and Remediation Systems covering 95,000 farmers in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana.
- 64,000+ Farm Development Plans issued.
- USD 136 million cumulative sustainability premiums paid to farmers and cooperatives via customer programs (https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/cocoa-compass-olam-cocoa-commits-to-living-incomes-for-farmers.html).

### 4.4 EUDR Compliance and Traceability

ofi reports 100% of farms in its direct supply chain mapped with GPS pins or polygons; the 2024 disclosure put polygon mapping at 79% of farms in sustainability programs, with the remainder geolocated by GPS pin (https://www.ofi.com/en-us/news-and-events/press-release/ofi-cocoa-shares-how-transparency-and-traceability-are-driving-sustainability-impact-for-food-and-beverage-sector.html). The 2025 CFI Progress Report confirms 100% polygon mapping across the direct supply chain and 730,000 farms now geolocated on ofi's EUDR-ready Track and Trace platform, with deforestation-risk assessments and action plans in all high-risk supply chains (https://assets.ctfassets.net/yzlb24ni1fxz/1pulqVam4Shl15FJUUKqQc/1acb2330ecfd9eac25274938c0920335/ofi_2025_CFI_Report.pdf). AtSource is ofi's customer-facing sustainability/traceability platform, and data flows into the EU TRACES DDS system. A 2024 innovation adds a Carbon Stock Monitoring tool (with NGIS) using AI to assess tree cover and deforestation risk. As of December 2024, ofi had conducted more than 135,000 deforestation assessments and registered 63,500 farms/cooperatives (https://news.mongabay.com/2024/12/agribusiness-giant-olam-gets-head-start-on-eudr-its-suppliers-not-so-much/).

---

## 5. Comparative Summary

### 5.1 Direct Sourcing and EUDR Mapping Completion

| Dimension | Barry Callebaut | Cargill | ofi (Olam) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct-share of total cocoa procurement | ~86% (CIV, 2024, Trase) | ~33% in Promise sub-supply chain (KPMG-audited) | ~60% direct (~40% indirect from other producers) |
| Farms polygon-mapped (most recent) | 1.5 million mapped (2024/25); 85.6% of direct plots mapped | 257,856 Promise farms (92.2% mapped); 99.9% CIV, 96% Ghana | 730,000 farms geolocated; 100% polygon-mapped in direct supply (2025) |
| Deforestation-free verified volumes | 81.8% of direct supply (2024/25) | Not disclosed in tonnage terms | 100% deforestation monitoring on direct farms since 2021 |
| Disclosed non-compliant volume exclusion | Indirect volume not meeting standards handled by supplier engagement | Explicit exclusion of farmers without full polygon maps or post-2014 deforestation | Suppliers failing the ofi Agri Supplier Code face remediation, suspension, or removal |

(https://trase.earth/insights/cote-d-ivoire-cocoa-exports-and-deforestation-2026; https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271059750/2024-impact-report-cocoa.pdf; https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/olam-cocoa-hits-100-percent-traceability-target-across-its-direct-global-supply-chain.html; https://www.barry-callebaut.com/system/files/2025-11/ESG%20Datasheet%202024_25_0.pdf; https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271058843/2024-impact-report-sustainable-supply-chain.pdf; https://assets.ctfassets.net/yzlb24ni1fxz/1pulqVam4Shl15FJUUKqQc/1acb2330ecfd9eac25274938c0920335/ofi_2025_CFI_Report.pdf)

### 5.2 Origin-Country Capex 2018–2024 (Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana)

| Company | Disclosed Capex |
|---|---|
| Barry Callebaut | CHF 55 million (~USD 56 million) SACO Abidjan (2019); 2022 Ghana solar expansion (0.64 MWp) |
| Cargill | ~USD 226 million (2019–2021): USD 100M Yopougon + USD 13M Tema + USD 12.3M sustainability/traceability + USD 13M farmer programs + USD 100M Yopougon completion |
| ofi | USD 75 million OCP San Pedro (2015); USD 200 million expansion announced 2024 |

(https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/about-us/media/news-stories/barry-callebaut-expands-its-cocoa-processing-capacities-cote-divoire; https://www.cargill.com/2019/cargill-invests-$113-million-in-ivory-coast-and-ghana; https://www.cargill.com/2021/cargill-completes-us$100m-cocoa-processing-expansion; https://www.cargill.com/2021/cargill-commissions-us$13-million-cocoa-processing-plant; https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/ouattara-olam-cocoa-processing-facility-cote-divoire.html)

### 5.3 Farmer Income Disclosures

| Company | Key Metrics |
|---|---|
| Barry Callebaut | 557,739 farmers above extreme poverty line; farm income 2021/22 ~XOF 1.35 million; <10% of farmers at a living income |
| Cargill | PROCOCO tripled farmer income from USD 72 to USD 239; sustainability premiums ~GH¢6 million/season in Ghana; 2025 target: 25,000 Ivorian households to close >50% of living-income gap |
| ofi | 30,168 farmers at a living income (2024); USD 136 million cumulative sustainability premiums paid; 95,000 farmers in CLMRS coverage |

(https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/sustainability/reporting/forever-chocolate-progress-report-202425; https://www.cargill.com/doc/1432227748042/a-decade-of-impact-in-cocoa-communities.pdf; https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/ofi-cocoa-shares-how-transparency-and-traceability-are-driving-sustainability-impact-for-food-and-beverage-sector.html)

---

## 6. Competitive Positioning in Premium Chocolate Contracts

All three processors appear as Tier 1 suppliers in Ferrero's 2022/23 and 2023/24 cocoa supplier disclosures, alongside ECOM, Touton, Sucden, and others (https://www.ferrero.com/int/sites/ferrero_int/files/2024-06/cocoa-supplier-list-22-23.pdf).

Barry Callebaut has the most diversified customer base and the deepest direct-supply chain (~86% direct from cooperatives in Côte d'Ivoire; 90% of Ivorian direct-sourcing cooperatives on three-year contracts). In 2023, BC announced a long-term extension of its supply partnership with Mondelēz in Belgium, including the acquisition of Mondelēz's Halle plant and a 30,000 t/year liquid-chocolate supply contract (https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/group/media/news-stories/barry-callebaut-extend-strategic-supply-partnership-mondelez-international). BC's 1.5 million mapped farms and EUDR leadership position make it the default B2B chocolate supplier for the largest branded manufacturers, with the deepest documented capital lock-in via plant acquisitions. However, the 2022 salmonella contamination crisis at the Wieze plant resulted in a multi-quarter volume loss at Mondelēz and prompted accelerated dual-sourcing discussions.

Cargill's USD 226 million disclosed West African capex (2019–2021) makes its Yopougon plant the largest single grinding facility in Africa, providing a structural cost advantage in cocoa powder supply for branded manufacturers needing high-volume, segregated, EUDR-ready West African cocoa. Its 2024 Impact Report discloses customer-specific programs (e.g., eight Ferrero pruning groups in Côte d'Ivoire benefitting 285 farmers; the Nestlé Income Accelerator partnership) (https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271059750/2024-impact-report-cocoa.pdf). CocoaWise™ is positioned as a customer EUDR-compliance tool.

ofi's competitive edge is its long-standing LBC presence in Ghana (~25% of national bean trade), 100% direct-chain traceability since 2020, and the AtSource digital platform, which Mondelēz, Nestlé, and others use to back sustainability claims. The 2021 Mondelēz–ofi Indonesia "world's largest sustainable commercial cocoa farm" partnership demonstrates its capacity to co-invest in farmer-income differentiation (https://ir.mondelezinternational.com/news-releases/news-release-details/mondelez-international-and-olam-food-ingredients-announce). ofi's 100% polygon mapping and Track and Trace integration with EU TRACES position it for the "transparent origin" segment of the premium chocolate market—particularly for customers whose EUDR compliance narrative requires segregated, traceable supply chains.

Lindt and Ferrero both publicly stated they pay the $400/tonne LID plus additional premiums through their own sustainability programs (https://www.ibtimes.com/africas-fields-plan-pay-fair-wages-chocolate-withers-3683134). The practical effect of EUDR readiness on contract competitiveness is that all three processors remain listed Tier 1 suppliers, but the strategic logic for each customer differs: Mondelēz's deepening tie-up with Barry Callebaut (Halle acquisition, 30,000 t/y liquid chocolate) suggests lock-in with the largest B2B player; Ferrero's continued use of all three indicates portfolio diversification across EUDR compliance models; and Lindt's sustainability-premium positioning aligns most naturally with ofi's transparency-first model.

---

## 7. Key Uncertainties and Data Gaps

1. Precise 2024 percentages of broker-purchased (indirect) cocoa are not disclosed uniformly across the three companies. The Trase estimate (86% direct for BC) and the Promise/ofi Direct percentages are not directly comparable because of different definitions (cooperatives, farmer organizations, LBC farmers, sustainability-program farmers).

2. Disclosed non-compliant volume exclusions (BC: indirect; Cargill: farmers without full polygon maps; ofi: supplier code remediation) are directional rather than quantified in tonnes; specific 2024 EUDR-ineligible volumes were not published in the sources gathered.

3. ofi's Ghana-specific capex 2018–2024 was not publicly itemized; most ofi capex disclosures in the period cover San Pedro, Côte d'Ivoire, and Indonesian BT Cocoa.

4. Premium chocolate contract values (Lindt, Ferrero, Mondelēz) are not disclosed; the available evidence is qualitative (joint ventures, customer-named program mentions, platform integrations).

5. Farmer income disclosure methodologies differ across the three companies: BC uses the World Bank extreme-poverty line; Cargill references USD 2/day; ofi uses the Anker living-income benchmark (USD 7,300/year in Côte d'Ivoire). Cross-company comparisons are therefore approximate.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Between 2018 and 2024, Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and ofi pursued substantively similar but quantitatively different vertical-integration strategies in West African cocoa: increased direct sourcing, expanded in-country processing capacity, intensified cooperative-level sustainability programming, and aggressive preparation for EUDR. Cargill's most measurable in-country capital deployment (USD 226 million over 2019–2021) is followed by ofi's USD 200 million 2024 expansion announcement. Barry Callebaut, while the largest chocolate manufacturer by revenue, does not break out West African capex in its public disclosures at the same granularity.

On EUDR, all three have publicly positioned themselves as ready, but the depth and timing of their public commitment differs: Barry Callebaut is the most vocal advocate and has the highest disclosed farm-mapping volume (1.5 million); Cargill has the most documented enterprise-wide multi-phase compliance program with explicit farmer-exclusion mechanisms; and ofi has the most granular customer-facing traceability platform (AtSource/Track and Trace) with 100% polygon mapping in its direct supply chain by 2025. None have disclosed EUDR-related volume exclusions in tonnage terms, which remains a material transparency gap.

For premium chocolate manufacturers, the practical effect is that all three processors remain listed Tier 1 suppliers, but contract competitiveness is increasingly determined by demonstrated EUDR readiness, origin-country processing depth, and proprietary farmer-income programming. Barry Callebaut retains the structural lead in premium outsourcing via scale and capital lock-in; Cargill is the most diversified across customer types with the largest single African grinding footprint; and ofi is the most origin-embedded with the strongest positioning for the segregated, fully-traceable segment of the premium market.

# How Edge Computing Is Transforming IoT Architectures in 2024: Three Industrial Use Cases

## Executive Summary

By 2024, edge computing has moved from an optimization technique to the default design pattern for latency-, bandwidth-, and availability-sensitive industrial IoT. The dominant 2010s architecture, in which sensors streamed raw data to a centralized cloud for analytics, has been replaced across manufacturing, robotics, and energy distribution by a **three-tier model**: sensors and actuators at the bottom, an edge compute tier (industrial PCs, on-board GPUs, MEC servers, or substation nodes) in the middle, and the cloud at the top retaining fleet analytics, model training, and global coordination.

This report examines the transformation across three industrial verticals, with before/after architecture diagrams, quantified impact metrics, and trade-off analysis grounded in real-world vendor deployments.

---

## 1. Manufacturing: Predictive Maintenance

### (a) Architecture: Cloud-Centric vs. Edge-Distributed

**Cloud-centric (legacy, ~pre-2018 pattern):**
Vibration, acoustic, current, and temperature sensors on CNC spindles, motors, and presses stream raw waveforms (often 10–25.6 kHz per channel) to a plant historian and then to a remote cloud data lake. ML inference (random forest, LSTM autoencoders, FFT analysis) runs in the cloud; alerts round-trip back to the shop floor via SCADA/DCS. The control loop physically traverses a wide-area network.

**Edge-distributed (2024 pattern):**
A three-tier stack has become standard:
1. *Sensing tier* — wireless multi-modal sensors at the asset (e.g., Nanoprecise's 6-in-1 MachineDoctor, ASUS AISSENS)
2. *Edge tier* — rugged industrial PCs (Premio JCO series on NVIDIA Jetson Orin, Siemens Industrial Edge, AWS IoT SiteWise Edge) running FFT + lightweight CNN inference at 25.6 kHz sampling
3. *Cloud tier* — fleet-wide analytics, model retraining, dashboards (Azure IoT Hub, AWS IoT Core)

A representative deployment: Frontmatec's food processing machinery uses Siemens Industrial Edge and AWS IoT SiteWise Edge, cutting deployment time "from several hours to just 15 minutes" while enabling local buffering across network outages.

### (b) Quantified Impacts

| Metric | Cloud-Centric (Before) | Edge-Distributed (2024) | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inference latency | 250–800 ms cloud round-trip | <20 ms on-prem; sub-15 ms with NVIDIA Jetson + TensorRT | Premio/StartMotionMedia; Oxmaint |
| Data bandwidth | Raw 25.6 kHz waveforms shipped upstream | 90–99% data reduction (Ademero: 90%; Layline.io: 99% on 10K sensors) | Ademero; Layline.io |
| Unplanned downtime | Reactive only | 30% reduction (US DOE); 70% (Global Manufacturing Corp.); 73% (EmbedCrest) | DOE; Ademero; EmbedCrest |
| Failure prediction lead time | Hours/days post-mortem | 48–72 h (Norvik, Bosch/Checkpluris); ~12 days (EmbedCrest CNN) | Norvik; Checkpluris; EmbedCrest |
| ROI | Negative (data lake costs) | Payback 10–14 months (McKinsey) | StartMotionMedia |

**Real-world deployment examples:**
- Nanoprecise detected a BPFO bearing outer-race failure at a Fortune 500 automaker, avoiding 8 hours of downtime.
- Nanoprecise at India's largest electrode manufacturer saved 16 hours of downtime and $187,000 with rotational-looseness detection.
- Norvik reports 48–72 h advance failure alerts at 91% precision on 8 production lines for a manufacturer previously losing $1.8 M/yr to unplanned downtime.
- EmbedCrest's NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano deployment across 340 CNC machines reduced unplanned downtime by 73% with a 94.2% accurate CNN and Modbus TCP automated shut-downs.
- Ademero/Global Manufacturing Corp. cut equipment failures by 70% and saved $4.2 M/yr using 25,000+ sensors with edge AI delivering <100 ms local processing and 90% bandwidth reduction.
- An automaker running 12 plants saved $12 M/yr with edge-AI predictive maintenance providing 48–72 h failure warning on 10,000+ sensors.

### (c) Trade-offs

**Cost:** CAPEX rises per line for rugged edge hardware (Premio JCO, Siemens IPC), but OPEX falls sharply. Layline.io's 10K-sensor example shows raw egress of 1.3 GB/sensor/month and $117/sensor/month cloud egress collapsing to 0.01 GB and $1.17 with edge filtering (a 99% reduction). Hybrid Edge Cloud studies report 75–80% combined energy and bandwidth cost reduction.

**Security:** The attack surface expands from one cloud to thousands of edge devices. Counter-measures include TPM 2.0 hardware root-of-trust (standard on Premio JCO), OTA firmware/model updates (SWUpdate, AWS IoT Greengrass), and network micro-segmentation (Cisco SD-Access in Audi's EC4P). The trade is acceptable when physical access to controllers is restricted but raises stakes in brownfield plants.

**Maintainability:** More endpoints to patch and update, but edge platforms (AWS IoT SiteWise Edge, Siemens Industrial Edge, Azure IoT Edge) provide centralized model/firmware lifecycle management. EmbedCrest highlights SWUpdate OTA as critical; Norvik's edge streaming pipeline required normalization across heterogeneous PLC/SCADA, a non-trivial one-time integration cost.

---

## 2. Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)

### (a) Architecture: Cloud-Centric vs. Edge-Distributed

**Cloud-centric (legacy):**
AMRs run SLAM on-board but offload path planning, fleet coordination, and sometimes perception to a remote cloud. The MiR (Mobile Industrial Robots) cloud planner reference design is canonical: TCP-based I/O-planner communication over Wi-Fi to a remote planner server. In dense warehouses, "50 AMRs on the floor will overload the traditional WiFi network. That leads to network latency and interrupts communication between AMRs and the WMS."

**Edge-distributed (2024 pattern):**
Three patterns dominate:
1. *On-device* — full autonomy on the robot (Bosch/Checkpluris, Ford Almussafes Survival 1.0 AIMW)
2. *Private/Public 5G + MEC* — control plane on a Multi-access Edge Compute node co-located with the 5G gNB (Makino on AWS Wavelength with KDDI, Tiny Mile on Bell Public MEC, LG CLOi CarryBot on private 5G)
3. *Local 5G with central brain* — Toshiba Local 5G server-centric swarm control with radio-map-based dynamic routing, demonstrating world-first real-time control of a 12-robot swarm

### (b) Quantified Impacts

| Metric | Cloud-Centric (Before) | Edge-Distributed (2024) | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control-loop latency | 200–500 ms cloud RTT on 4G/Wi-Fi; >100 ms video over Wi-Fi 6 (123 ms video / 13.85 ms audio benchmark) | 9–19.92 ms with 5G MEC; 1 ms target with URLLC | Sheridan Tech; Liang/Ramirez 2023; MDPI 2023; hospital AMR study |
| Handoff reliability | Wi-Fi AP handoffs cause "rogue robots"; frequent dropped commands | "No communication disconnections even when a moving object communicated across multiple 5G base station areas" | Makino/AWS |
| AMR availability | Variable | 99.5% uptime (TCL/ForwardX) | ForwardX |
| Operational cost | Baseline | 20% cost savings (TCL) | ForwardX |
| Throughput | Baseline | +30% throughput capacity (TCL) | ForwardX |

**Real-world deployment examples:**
- **TCL + ForwardX (world's first 5G AMR project).** 12 Flex 300-LS AMRs achieved 99.5% error-free uptime, 20% operational cost savings, and 30% increase in throughput capacity, repurposing 34 workers.
- **Makino (Japan) + AWS Wavelength/KDDI.** Switched from factory servers to AWS Wavelength over Public 5G in <5 months, achieving stable connectivity across base-station handovers. URLLC 5G can push AMR planner latency to ~1 ms on average.
- **Tiny Mile (Toronto) + Bell Public MEC / AWS Wavelength.** Offloads heavy obstacle-detection computation from rovers to MEC for real-time collision avoidance.
- **Nokia + MiR private 5G.** First demonstration of private 5G AMR fleet management at LogiMat 2022.
- **Ford España Survival 1.0 AIMW.** First autonomous industrial mobile warehouse, deployed to replace 170 fixed spare-parts warehouses in the Almussafes plant.
- **Hospital AMR study (2024).** 5G averaged 19.92 ms latency (σ 0.17) versus higher and more variable Wi-Fi, with sub-1 ms targets needed for autonomous navigation.
- **5G MEC in smart factories.** 5G+MEC delivered average downlink >746 Mbps, average latency 16 ms (low 9 ms), and average jitter 0.27 ms.

### (c) Trade-offs

**Cost:** Private 5G CAPEX (small cells, core, SIM/eSIM management) is significant. Makino explicitly chose Public 5G + AWS Wavelength to avoid Private 5G buildout. On-device GPUs are power-hungry and drain batteries, forcing robots off the road during peak hours, the rationale for Tiny Mile's offloading. Private 5G in U.S. warehouses is increasingly viable via CBRS spectrum, "dramatically lowering the barrier to entry."

**Security:** MEC keeps sensitive video and SLAM data on-premises or at the carrier edge rather than the public cloud (a privacy win), but the radio and edge node become new attack surfaces. Nokia/MiR/Continental deployments use private APNs, SIM-based device identity, and isolated MEC zones. The Berkeley offload study highlights that "even a few tens of milliseconds of additional latency can drop the accuracy of manipulation by over 10%," so offload decisions require careful task partitioning.

**Maintainability:** Fleet management software (ForwardX f(x), Continental's AWS-backed stack, SVT Robotics middleware for LG) abstracts heterogeneous AMRs, but integrating with legacy WMS remains non-trivial. MEC application lifecycle (containerized AI modules) requires DevOps skills that traditional WMS integrators often lack. Maintainability is materially harder for AMRs than for stationary PdM because the safety case is in the loop; a bad model update can be a safety incident, not just a bug.

---

## 3. Energy Distribution

### (a) Architecture: Cloud-Centric vs. Edge-Distributed

**Cloud-centric (legacy SCADA):**
Substation Remote Terminal Units (RTUs) and Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) report to a centralized Distribution Management System (DMS) / SCADA in the control center via DNP3 or IEC 61850. Decisions, including FLISR (Fault Location, Isolation, Service Restoration), are made centrally. Legacy DNP3 introduces "larger time delays" into sectionalizing.

**Edge-distributed (2024 pattern):**
Two patterns now coexist, often combined:
1. *Substation edge node + protocol translation* — industrial edge nodes (Barbara, FledgePOWER, AWS IoT Greengrass on rugged gateways) sit at the substation, translating legacy protocols (Modbus, IEC-104, IEC-61850, DNP3, GOOSE, SOAP, OPC-UA, FTP) into a normalized stream (MQTT/Pub-Sub), performing real-time analytics and local decisions.
2. *Virtualized Protection and Control (VPAC)* — substation protection logic runs inside a virtualized real-time hypervisor (LF Energy SEAPATH on KVM/QEMU with preempt-RT Linux) on commodity edge hardware.

The rationale: "traditional Cloud solutions fall short due to latency, cost, and limited scalability" for thousands of geographically dispersed transformation centers.

### (b) Quantified Impacts

| Metric | Cloud-Centric (Before) | Edge-Distributed (2024) | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trip / protection latency | Seconds (centralized SCADA/DNP3) | 4–5 ms round-trip for GOOSE protection schemes; <3 ms IEC 61850 trip; sub-4 ms GOOSE budget | National Grid SEAPATH; MDPI GOOSE survey |
| Data reduction at edge | ~100% raw telemetry to DMS | 60–90% reduction; 40–80% typical | WJAETS 2025; Frontiers 2024 |
| Outage frequency (SAIFI) | Baseline | 15–25% reduction after edge-cloud deployment | WJAETS 2025 |
| Outage duration (SAIDI) | Baseline | 20–40% reduction after edge-cloud deployment | WJAETS 2025 |
| Equipment failure rate | Reactive | 25–47% reduction with AI predictive maintenance | WJAETS 2025 |
| Time to restoration | Minutes to hours (manual dispatch) | Seconds (FLISR GOOSE peer-to-peer); up to 50% fewer site trips | SEL; Iberdrola |

**Real-world deployment examples:**
- **Iberdrola / i-DE SmartPoint (AWS IoT + Greengrass, 2024).** Asset management platform for "millions of operational assets" including poles, transformation centers, substations, lines, and field workers. "i-DE has reduced reported incidents by 40 percent and cut the number of trips needed to facilities by 50 percent."
- **NGET (UK) + GE Vernova + LF Energy SEAPATH (VPAC).** Open-source hypervisor achieved 4–5 ms round-trip GOOSE latency for protection schemes; demonstrated 25+ Sampled-Value streams per VM, CPU isolation, IRQ offloading, PCI passthrough, clustering/failover, and remote injection testing.
- **RTE (France TSO) + LF Energy FledgePOWER/Dianomic FogLAMP.** Deployed in RTE's 2,600 substations for multi-protocol translation, offering "significant cost reduction and complexity vs migrating the existing RTUs."
- **Cuerva (Spanish DSO) + Barbara.** Edge node per substation ingesting Modbus RTU, IEC-104, IEC-61850, SOAP, OPC-UA, FTP; enabled substation digital twins, proactive maintenance, and an AI Flexibility market.
- **REDEIA (Spain TSO) + Barbara.** Virtualized oscilloscope at the substation edge: 4,000 voltage/current samples/second, GOOSE event-triggered capture, replacing dedicated physical oscilloscopes.
- **SCE (California) 5G FAN.** Reducing "data transfer delays from a few minutes" (NetComm) to "a few seconds" (5G FAN) for 250,000+ connected devices represents roughly two orders of magnitude improvement, with network slicing reserving <8 ms latency for teleprotection channels.
- **NREL 5G-SER distributed control.** Grid-edge test achieved sub-100 ms DCS polling with MEC + 5G; network survivability during power outage increased "from hours to a duration of many weeks" via microgrid-backed 5G core.

### (c) Trade-offs

**Cost:** Edge nodes (Barbara Industrial Edge, FledgePOWER on commodity hardware, SEAPATH hypervisors) avoid the larger cost and operational risk of replacing every legacy RTU; RTE explicitly cites this advantage. However, the DSO/TSO must now manage thousands of edge devices with firmware, certificates, and models. SCE's modernization is capital-intensive: $119M on CBRS priority access licenses and >800 RAN sites across 50,000 sq mi, but reduces long-term OPEX by retiring proprietary mesh radio.

**Security:** Distributed IEDs and edge nodes enlarge the attack surface of critical infrastructure, the most acute trade-off across the three use cases. Counter-measures include IEC 62443-style zoning, hardware root-of-trust (TPM), signed firmware/OTA, and microsegmentation. Fledge and SEAPATH leverage open-source transparency for vendor neutrality, but the operator owns more of the security lifecycle. NERC CIP and IEC 62443 compliance adds audit overhead but also enforces a high security standard.

**Maintainability:** Protocol translation (FledgePOWER, Barbara connectors) dramatically simplifies integration of heterogeneous fleets, but VPAC virtual machines require specialized real-time Linux expertise. Substation-edge devices are physically remote, often in unmanned substations, so remote management, signed firmware, and automated recovery are non-negotiable. Iberdrola's i-DE manages this at "millions of operational assets" scale via AWS IoT Core + Greengrass fleet management.

---

## Cross-Cutting Synthesis

1. **Latency is the headline gain.** Across all three use cases, edge computing converts hundreds of milliseconds of cloud round-trip into single-digit or sub-millisecond local response: <20 ms for predictive maintenance, 1–20 ms for AMR control loops, 4–5 ms for substation GOOSE protection. The IEC 61850 sub-4 ms protection budget is now routinely met by virtualized edge platforms, a regime that cloud-centric SCADA could never satisfy.

2. **Bandwidth reduction of 60–99% is typical.** Whether it's filter-and-aggregate pipelines (Layline.io 99%, Ademero 90%) or substation protocol translation (WJAETS 60–90%), the volume of bytes traversing the WAN drops by roughly an order of magnitude, materially changing unit economics for cellular backhaul and rural distribution assets.

3. **Availability and SLA improvements are reported as 15–50% gains.** Iberdrola cut reported incidents by 40% and site trips by 50%; NGET demonstrated clustering/failover; FLISR peer-to-peer GOOSE restores supply in seconds versus hours. The cloud-centric model is fundamentally more vulnerable because its single point of failure is the central SCADA/DMS link.

4. **Cost, security, and maintainability trade-offs favor edge for mission-critical loops but increase lifecycle complexity.** Hybrid Edge Cloud studies quantify 75–80% combined energy/bandwidth savings, but every additional edge node is another device to patch, harden, and integrate. Vendor case studies consistently show success when the edge platform is paired with strong fleet management (AWS IoT Greengrass, Siemens Industrial Edge, Cisco SD-Access, Fledge/FogLAMP, LF Energy SEAPATH).

5. **Open-source momentum is real in energy, more limited elsewhere.** LF Energy's Fledge/SEAPATH and the FledgePOWER/FogLAMP commercial layer are reshaping substation architectures with multi-vendor, hardware-agnostic stacks (RTE, NGET, JEA, Statkraft). Manufacturing and AMR remain dominated by commercial stacks (Siemens/AWS, Cisco/Audi, Nokia/MiR, AWS Wavelength, NVIDIA/Premio), but the same architectural pattern is winning across all three verticals.

In short, by 2024 the edge-distributed, three-tier IoT architecture (device → edge node/MEC → cloud) is the consensus answer for predictive maintenance, AMRs, and energy distribution because it converts millisecond-scale control loops from impossible to routine, reduces upstream bandwidth by 1–2 orders of magnitude, and demonstrably improves reliability and SLA, at the price of a larger distributed footprint that must be managed with industrial-grade security, lifecycle, and observability tooling.

Based on extensive research, no 5K race with the literal words "bubble gum" in its title has been confirmed at California's Great America theme park in Santa Clara.

The race you are most likely thinking of is the **Bubba Gump Shrimp Co.'s "Run Forrest Run" 5K Fun Run** — a Forrest Gump–themed 5K that was actually hosted at California's Great America (4701 Great America Parkway, Santa Clara, CA) during the mid-to-late 2000s. The phrase "Bubba Gump" is phonetically very close to "bubble gum," which likely accounts for the mix-up.

The race was sponsored by the Bubba Gump Shrimp Company, sent runners through the park walkways and surrounding parking lot, and typically included a complimentary day-pass to Great America, a finisher medal, and a post-race Bubba Gump shrimp meal.
